segments.title: King William's War and the contest for Canada
segments.summary: The Battle of Hudson Bay is placed within King William's War, the under-resourced North American struggle connected to the Nine Years' War.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville and explains why the 1697 action belongs to a wider Anglo-French contest for Canada. European governments supplied few additional resources, leaving commercial interests and their local partners to carry much of the conflict. The result was warfare shaped as much by trade networks and company power as by formal state strategy.

evidence.note: The lecture identifies d'Iberville, King William's War, the Nine Years' War, and the commercial character of the Canadian conflict.

segments.title: How the Hudson's Bay Company controlled trade
segments.summary: A look at the Hudson's Bay Company's trading monopoly, its unequal exchange system, and the fortified posts called factories.
segments.body: The company dominated the regional supply chain: imported food, ammunition, clothing, and other necessities passed through its hands, while furs flowed back toward Europe. Dr. Clarke stresses that transport cost and risk were real but could also justify heavily padded margins. Its factories were trading posts with varying degrees of fortification and manpower, built to secure a lucrative commercial network in a sparsely populated region.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the company's economic control, trade margins, and fortified factory system.

segments.title: d'Iberville's education and early maritime career
segments.summary: d'Iberville's Jesuit education, family connections, seagoing experience, and fur-trade work prepared him unusually early for command.
segments.body: By 1697 d'Iberville was only about thirty-five but already had extensive campaigning behind him. His classical and religious education was followed by service aboard family ships and work in the Canadian fur trade. Dr. Clarke argues that the ease of this transition suggests a capable young man being prepared for the family enterprise rather than a firmly intended priest abandoning his vocation.

evidence.note: The transcript traces d'Iberville's education, family shipping experience, fur-trade work, and early promise.

segments.title: The Compagnie du Nord privatizes the struggle for Hudson Bay
segments.summary: French commercial competition turned into company warfare as d'Iberville and his brothers seized English posts and ships around James Bay.
segments.body: The Hudson's Bay Company diverted the fur trade away from Quebec, prompting the French-backed Compagnie du Nord to compete in the bay. In an era when chartered companies could maintain troops and armed ships, this commercial rivalry readily became warfare. The 1686 expedition captured the English posts on James Bay, and d'Iberville later took additional company vessels while trying to sustain the isolated conquests with minimal state support.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the French company response, the 1686 expedition, captured posts, and later prizes.

segments.title: Newfoundland raids reveal the limits of company power
segments.summary: d'Iberville's destructive Newfoundland campaign shows both his operational skill and how quickly regular state forces could overwhelm company-scale warfare.
segments.body: After further campaigning, d'Iberville attacked English settlements on Newfoundland before being diverted to York Factory in 1697. Dr. Clarke presents him as an experienced, improvisational commander who often chose weakly defended targets and inflicted severe destruction. The arrival of roughly two thousand English troops after his departure illustrates the gulf between formidable chartered companies and the far greater force a state could deploy.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the Newfoundland raids, diversion to York Factory, and the arrival of a much larger English state force.

segments.title: Pelican and the French squadron bound for York Factory
segments.summary: The French expedition combined d'Iberville's heavily armed flagship Pelican with frigates, a converted store ship, and other support vessels.
segments.body: Pelican carried forty-four guns and sat on the changing late-seventeenth-century boundary between rates, while the wider force mixed combatants and logistical vessels. Heavy fog separated the flagship from the rest of the squadron as it approached York Factory. This left d'Iberville advancing alone at the moment the opposing English ships appeared.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Pelican's armament, the supporting French vessels, and the squadron's separation in fog.

segments.title: Pelican fights three English ships in Hudson Bay
segments.summary: Outnumbered and badly damaged, Pelican survives when a broadside detonates Hampshire's magazine and transforms the battle.
segments.body: d'Iberville initially mistook the approaching sails for his missing squadron, then discovered an English force led by the fifty-gun Hampshire and accompanied by two Hudson's Bay Company ships. The three-to-one engagement produced brutal close-range broadsides, with Pelican apparently near defeat. Hampshire's magazine then exploded; Daring escaped and Hudson's Bay surrendered, although the shattered Pelican was itself lost before the rest of the French force arrived.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the opposing ships and recounts the engagement, Hampshire's explosion, the surrender, and Pelican's loss.

segments.title: The capture of York Factory exposes a garrison problem
segments.summary: York Factory fell within eight days, demonstrating why remote commercial posts needed stronger fortifications, larger garrisons, and costly imported support.
segments.body: The French siege quickly captured a post whose normal population was only around fifty or sixty people. Dr. Clarke connects such losses to later British pressure for larger colonial garrisons, while emphasizing the financial burden where local populations could not sustain a militia. York Factory also lacked the surrounding population and infrastructure that let the East India Company acquire, remodel, and supply stronger positions elsewhere.

evidence.note: The transcript covers York Factory's fall, garrison requirements, militia economics, fortification, and supply constraints.

segments.title: Why corporate navies remain subordinate to states
segments.summary: A closing thought experiment asks how modern corporate fleets might protect trade, before arguing that governments ultimately suppress rivals to sovereign power.
segments.body: The lecture ends by imagining a world in which successors to the great chartered companies could still establish monopolies, wage war, and maintain navies against piracy. Dr. Clarke argues that this model was probably doomed: governments may welcome revenue from powerful companies, but will turn against them once they become competitors for political and military authority.

evidence.note: The transcript poses the corporate-navy counterfactual and argues that states destroy companies that rival sovereign power.
